message.content:
Adversaries may clear system logs on Linux or macOS to hide evidence of their activities, a technique known as Indicator Removal: Clear Linux or Mac System Logs. System logs, typically stored under the /var/log/ directory, record system and user-initiated actions. By deleting these logs, adversaries can obscure traces of their intrusion, making detection and forensic analysis more challenging. Notable malware such as MacMa, Proton, Rocke, and TeamTNT have been observed employing this technique, targeting logs in directories like /var/log/ and /Library/logs.

The potential impact includes the loss of critical forensic data, hindering incident response efforts and prolonging the time adversaries can remain undetected within a system. Common exploitation methods involve using native commands or scripts to delete or overwrite log files.

Mitigation strategies include implementing centralized logging to ensure logs are stored in a secure, remote location, making it harder for adversaries to tamper with them. Additionally, employing file integrity monitoring can alert administrators to unauthorized changes to log files, and ensuring proper access controls can limit the ability of adversaries to manipulate logs.
